nativeRange.habitat: Sandy patches and rubble flats adjacent to coral reefs at depths generally between 1 and 30 m, usually hovering near the entrance of a burrow shared with a pistol shrimp.

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5-8 cm deep for burrowing
loose coral rubble for burrow construction
live rock for hiding and territorial perches
open sandy footprint near the front glass for viewing

diet.notes: Carnivorous; generally accepts a varied meaty diet of frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped table shrimp, and small carnivore pellets or flakes. Has been reported to do best when fed small portions at least twice daily. Newly imported specimens may refuse food for several days after introduction but typically transition onto prepared foods once settled.

goodWith: small peaceful reef fish (firefish, cardinalfish, royal grammas)
avoidWith: large aggressive predators (groupers, lionfish, large angelfish)
boisterous bottom-dwellers that disturb the burrow
triggerfish and other crustacean hunters (will eat the partner shrimp)
additional conspecifics in small tanks (occasional quarrelling reported)

summary: A small, peaceful shrimp goby with a white body, dark diagonal bands, a yellow face, and a tall filament-tipped dorsal fin. Forms a well-documented burrow partnership with pistol shrimp on sandy reef beds.
careNotes:
The Hi Fin Red Banded Goby is one of the most popular symbiotic shrimp gobies in the marine hobby. It is best known for its mutualistic relationship with pistol shrimp — typically Randall's Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus randalli), though Tiger Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus bellulus) have also been observed pairing with it in captivity. The shrimp excavates and maintains a burrow while the goby stands guard at the entrance, watching for predators.

To support this behaviour, an aquascape with a fine sand bed of at least 5-8 cm and a few flat pieces of rubble for the shrimp to build under is generally advised. Open sandy floor near the front of the tank lets the pair be observed.

Cross-source sizing is consistent: LiveAquaria lists 2 inches (~5 cm) and reeflings.com cites 6 cm; the 5-6.5 cm recorded range averages these. Water parameters agree closely across sources (LiveAquaria 22-26°C, reeflings.com 24-28°C); the listed 23-27°C is the cross-source average. Tank-size recommendations vary: LiveAquaria cites 10 US gal (~38 L) and reeflings.com 40 L, but those figures barely accommodate a dedicated sand footprint plus rockwork for the pair. The listed 75 L minimum biases toward the larger side per AquaMate's cautious tank-size policy.

A tightly-fitting lid is important — like most gobies and dartfish, S. nematodes has been reported to jump when startled. The species is generally considered fully reef-safe and ignores corals and ornamental invertebrates. Captive-bred specimens are commercially available and tend to acclimate more readily than wild-caught fish.

Usually kept singly or as a heterosexual pair. Multiple individuals in a small tank have been reported to occasionally quarrel over burrow territory; in larger systems several can be kept together. Avoid housing with crustacean-eating predators that would target the pistol-shrimp partner.
breedingNotes: Has been spawned in captivity but is generally considered difficult to rear. Pairs deposit eggs inside the shared burrow and the male tends the clutch. Raising the pelagic larvae requires very small live foods (rotifers, then copepod nauplii) and remains the main hurdle for hobbyist breeding success.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species page navigated from the gobies collection root supplied by the manifest. Primary source for hobby water parameters, tank minimum, temperament, diet, and care level.

additional.name: reeflings.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size, water parameters, native range, reef safety, and pistol-shrimp partnership. Provided wild depth (1-30 m) and the jumping/lid warning.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been bred in captivity but rearing the pelagic larvae is the main challenge; live rotifers and copepod nauplii are generally required.
